nativeRange.habitat: Seasonal wetlands, marshes, and riverbanks across tropical West and Central Africa

decorPreferences: open background placement with room to grow tall
bright lighting to develop red coloration

summary: Red Ammannia (Ammannia gracilis) is a tall, demanding stem plant from tropical West and Central Africa, valued for the wavy copper-red to pink foliage it develops under high light, pressurized CO2, and iron-rich fertilization. Without those conditions it tends to stay pale yellow-green.
careNotes:
Red Ammannia is sold in the hobby under several names — 'Large Ammania', 'Pink Ammania', and 'Delicate Ammania' are all commonly applied to Ammannia gracilis. It grows larger-leaved and more upright than its smaller relative Ammannia senegalensis, and is typically placed as a background accent in tanks 60 cm or longer. The species comes from seasonal wetlands, marshes, and riverbanks in tropical West and Central Africa, including Senegal, Mali, Guinea, and neighbouring countries. Water parameters are broadly agreed to fall in the range of 22–28 °C, pH 6.0–7.5, and soft to moderately hard water, though some sources extend the tolerated pH range further in either direction.

To develop the copper-red to pink coloration the plant is known for, it generally needs high light in the range of 30–50 PAR over an 8–10 hour photoperiod, pressurized CO2 at around 25–30 mg/L, and consistent dosing of iron and micronutrients alongside regular macro fertilization. Under lower light it tends to survive but stays pale yellow-green and may drop its lower leaves over time. The best red colour is reported when nitrate levels run low while phosphate and micronutrients remain plentiful. A nutrient-rich substrate — aquasoil or a standard substrate supplemented with root tabs — is preferred. The plant is sensitive to sudden parameter swings, which can trigger leaf melt, and this is especially common in freshly planted tissue-culture stems adjusting from the air-grown (emersed) form to the underwater (submersed) form.
breedingNotes: Propagated by stem cuttings: cut the newest growth (about 5-10 cm with a few leaves) from a mature stem and replant in the substrate. Trimming the upper half of an established stem encourages bushier lateral side-shoot growth on the mother plant. When grown emersed, the plant produces small purple flowers, but seed propagation is uncommon in the hobby.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Used for family (Lythraceae), West African origin, care level (medium-difficult), light (high), CO2 (recommended, 25-30 ppm), growth rate (fast), placement (mid-background), pH (5-7.5, soft acidic preferred), propagation, and iron/micro fertilization. Primary role assigned to Buce Plant because the Tropica search URL supplied in the manifest returned no per-species detail page for Ammannia gracilis — Tropica does not currently appear to list this species (their Ammannia catalogue page shows A. crassicaulis instead).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-checked placement (background), growth rate (fast), height (>10 in / >25 cm), light/CO2 producing red-to-pink coloration vs yellow-green under medium light, and Easy Green / iron fertilization.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (aquariumbreeder.com)
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (West/Central Africa, Senegal, Mali, Guinea, etc.), adult size (25-50 cm), temperature (22-26 °C), pH (6.5-7.5), GH (2-10), light (30-50 PAR), CO2 (required), minimum tank size (~80 L / 20 gal), and propagation by 5-8 cm cuttings.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Lythraceae), West African origin (Senegal wetlands/marshes), size (20-50 cm), temperature (20-28 °C), pH (6-8), hardness (4-12 dGH), high light, CO2 supplementation, gravel/nutrient substrate, and 10 cm stem-cutting propagation.
